meta.title: Eduversal School Appraisal Framework
meta.description: A five-domain institutional self-appraisal framework for Eduversal partner schools. Aligned with CIS, WASC, NEASC, and Danielson-informed practices. Schools complete a self-assessment; external evaluators from Eduversal validate and provide commendations and recommendations.
references: CIS (Council of International Schools) School Evaluation
WASC (Western Association of Schools and Colleges) Self-Study
NEASC (New England Association of Schools and Colleges)

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Practice is absent, inconsistent, or at an early stage. Little or no evidence exists.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Practice is emerging and partially implemented. Some evidence exists but gaps remain.

3.label: Established
3.description: Practice is consistently implemented with clear evidence. Meets expected standards.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Practice is fully embedded, innovative, and can serve as a model for others.

meta.cycle: Annual self-appraisal by school, followed by external validation visit within the same academic year.

domains.title: Leadership & Governance
domains.description: Evaluates the quality of school leadership, strategic planning, governance structures, and the use of data and evidence in decision-making. Strong leadership provides clear direction, builds institutional culture, and ensures accountability at all levels.

aspects.title: Vision, Mission & Strategic Direction
aspects.description: The school has a clearly articulated vision and mission that guide all institutional decisions. A Strategic Development Plan (SDP) with SMART goals is in place and actively monitored.
expectations: The school's vision and mission are documented, publicly accessible, and reviewed periodically.
A Strategic Development Plan (SDP) or School Improvement Plan (SIP) exists with clear goals, timelines, and responsible parties.
Goals are SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) and tracked with progress indicators.
Leadership team reviews SDP progress at least twice per academic year and adjusts plans accordingly.
Staff, students, and parents are aware of and can articulate the school's mission and core values.
evidence_examples: Published vision and mission statements (website, handbook)
Strategic Development Plan (SDP) document with SMART goals
SDP progress review meeting minutes
Staff survey results on mission awareness
Action plan with timelines and ownership assignments

aspects.title: Leadership Structure & Accountability
aspects.description: The school has a clear organisational structure with defined roles and responsibilities. Leadership is distributed, and accountability mechanisms are in place.
expectations: An up-to-date organisational chart clearly defines roles, reporting lines, and responsibilities.
Job descriptions exist for all leadership positions and are reviewed annually.
A KPI system is in place for evaluating principal and leadership team performance.
Leadership decisions are transparent and communicated to relevant stakeholders.
The school conducts regular leadership self-evaluation and uses results to drive improvement.
evidence_examples: Organisational chart (current academic year)
Job descriptions for principal, vice-principals, and heads of department
KPI evaluation forms and completed appraisal records for leadership
Leadership meeting minutes and decision logs
Leadership self-evaluation reports

aspects.title: Policy Framework & Compliance
aspects.description: The school operates within a documented policy framework aligned with national regulations and Cambridge requirements. Policies are reviewed, communicated, and enforced.
expectations: Core policies (academic integrity, safeguarding, admissions, staff conduct, data protection) are documented and accessible.
Policies are reviewed at least every two years and updated as needed.
Staff, students, and parents are informed of relevant policies through handbooks or onboarding.
The school complies with Indonesian national education regulations (BAN-S/M, Dinas Pendidikan requirements).
Cambridge Centre requirements are met and documented.
evidence_examples: Staff handbook and student-parent handbook
Policy register with review dates
Safeguarding policy and emergency response plan
Cambridge Centre compliance documentation
Legal compliance records (institutional permits, operating licences)

aspects.title: Data-Informed Decision Making
aspects.description: The school systematically collects and uses data to inform decisions at all levels — from classroom practice to strategic planning.
expectations: The school collects and analyses academic performance data at least once per term.
Stakeholder feedback (surveys from parents, students, and staff) is gathered and acted upon.
Data is used to set targets, identify gaps, and monitor progress over time.
Leadership shares data insights with staff and uses findings to prioritise professional development.
Student wellbeing and attendance data are tracked and linked to intervention plans.
evidence_examples: Academic performance dashboards or trend reports
Parent and student satisfaction survey results and response actions
Staff wellbeing survey data and follow-up actions
Data-driven action plans from leadership meetings
Attendance and pastoral intervention records

aspects.title: Financial Governance & Resource Management
aspects.description: The school manages its financial and physical resources responsibly, transparently, and in alignment with its educational priorities.
expectations: An annual budget is developed and linked to the school's strategic priorities.
Financial reports are reviewed by leadership and governing body at regular intervals.
Resource allocation decisions (staffing, facilities, technology) are documented and justified.
The school maintains adequate physical resources to support quality teaching and learning.
Procurement and financial processes comply with applicable regulations.
evidence_examples: Annual budget plan and actuals comparison
Financial governance meeting minutes
Resource audit reports (library, IT, laboratory equipment)
Capital expenditure justification records
Facilities maintenance plan and inspection records

domains.title: Teaching & Learning Quality
domains.description: Evaluates the quality of classroom practice, teacher effectiveness, use of assessment in learning, and integration of technology as an instructional tool. Aligned with Danielson Framework domains 1–3.

aspects.title: Lesson Planning & Preparation
aspects.description: Teachers plan lessons that are purposeful, differentiated, and aligned with curriculum objectives. Planning demonstrates knowledge of content, students, and pedagogical approach.
expectations: Lesson plans are structured, evidence-based, and reference curriculum objectives or Cambridge syllabus points.
Plans include differentiation strategies for diverse learners (SEN, EAL, gifted and talented).
Unit or scheme of work documents exist and are used to ensure curriculum coherence.
Teachers use formative assessment data from previous lessons to inform planning.
Resources and materials selected are appropriate, engaging, and high quality.
evidence_examples: Sample lesson plans from multiple subjects and year groups
Schemes of work or unit plans
Differentiation strategies documentation
Assessment-to-planning feedback cycles (records of how data influenced next lesson)
Resource and material audit

aspects.title: Classroom Environment & Culture
aspects.description: Classrooms are safe, inclusive, and intellectually stimulating environments where students feel respected, challenged, and supported.
expectations: Classrooms demonstrate a positive and respectful learning environment observable during walkthroughs.
Routines and expectations are clearly established and consistently maintained.
Students are actively engaged and take intellectual risks without fear of failure.
Inclusive practices are evident — all students have opportunities to participate meaningfully.
Classroom displays and resources reflect diverse perspectives and support learning goals.
evidence_examples: Classroom observation records or walkthrough notes
Student engagement survey results
Photographs of learning environment and displays
Incident and behaviour data showing positive trends
Student and parent feedback on classroom experience

aspects.title: Instructional Practice & Pedagogy
aspects.description: Teachers use a repertoire of evidence-based instructional strategies to maximise student understanding and engagement.
expectations: Observed lessons demonstrate varied instructional strategies (direct instruction, inquiry, collaborative learning, etc.).
Teachers check for understanding throughout lessons using formative techniques.
Questioning is purposeful — teachers use higher-order questions that promote critical thinking.
Feedback to students is timely, specific, and actionable.
Teachers reflect on their own practice and are responsive to student needs in real time.
evidence_examples: Observation forms completed by HoDs or academic coordinators
Peer observation records
Sample student work with teacher feedback annotations
Video recordings of lessons (with consent)
Teacher reflection journals or post-lesson review notes

aspects.title: Assessment for Learning
aspects.description: Assessment is integral to teaching and learning. Teachers use both formative and summative assessments to monitor progress, inform instruction, and provide meaningful feedback.
expectations: A school-wide assessment policy defines types, frequency, and purpose of assessments.
Formative assessment tools (exit tickets, quizzes, self-assessment) are used regularly in lessons.
Summative assessments are moderated across teachers for consistency.
Assessment data is used to identify students needing additional support or extension.
Students understand assessment criteria and are involved in self and peer assessment processes.
evidence_examples: School assessment policy document
Sample formative assessment tools and records
Moderation records (inter-rater reliability evidence)
Mark books / gradebooks showing trend data
Student self-assessment and peer feedback samples

aspects.title: Technology Integration in Teaching
aspects.description: Educational technology is purposefully integrated to enhance learning outcomes, not merely for engagement. Teachers are competent and confident technology users.
expectations: The school has a Learning Management System (LMS) that is actively used by teachers and students.
Teachers integrate digital tools that support learning objectives (not for substitution alone — SAMR model awareness).
LMS analytics or usage data are reviewed to monitor engagement and identify gaps.
Teachers receive regular professional development on educational technology.
A digital citizenship programme is in place for students.
evidence_examples: LMS usage analytics (active courses, submission rates, engagement metrics)
EdTech professional development records
SAMR-level technology integration evidence in lesson observations
Digital citizenship curriculum or programme documents
Technology inventory and maintenance records

domains.title: Curriculum & Academic Programmes
domains.description: Evaluates the breadth, coherence, and quality of the school's curriculum — including Cambridge programme delivery, subject department organisation, and professional development of teaching staff.

aspects.title: Curriculum Design & Coherence
aspects.description: The school's curriculum is well-designed, vertically and horizontally aligned, and prepares students for Cambridge qualifications and lifelong learning.
expectations: A written curriculum framework maps learning outcomes across all year groups and subjects.
Curriculum is aligned with Cambridge International syllabuses and learning frameworks.
Vertical alignment ensures progression from primary through secondary and into IGCSE/A-Level.
Cross-curricular connections are identified and planned for within units of work.
The curriculum is reviewed annually and updated based on new syllabus versions and school data.
evidence_examples: Curriculum map (scope and sequence) per subject and year group
Cambridge syllabus alignment documents
Cross-curricular planning records or project-based learning units
Curriculum review meeting minutes and revision history
Ofqual / Cambridge Centre compliance evidence

aspects.title: Subject Department Structure & Effectiveness
aspects.description: Subject departments are well-organised, with active leadership and regular collaborative practice among teachers.
expectations: Each department has a designated Head of Department (HoD) with clear responsibilities.
Departmental meetings occur regularly with documented agendas and minutes.
Heads of Department coordinate across departments to ensure curriculum coherence.
Department improvement plans are linked to school-wide strategic goals.
HoDs conduct regular classroom observations and provide feedback to teachers.
evidence_examples: Department structure charts with member lists
HoD meeting minutes (at least monthly)
Cross-department coordination meeting records
Department improvement plans
HoD observation forms and feedback records

aspects.title: Teacher Appraisal & Professional Development
aspects.description: The school has a structured teacher appraisal system and provides meaningful professional development opportunities linked to individual and school-wide growth goals.
expectations: All teachers complete a structured self-evaluation and set professional development goals annually.
Formal appraisal cycles include classroom observation, evidence review, and a professional dialogue.
Professional development is aligned to school improvement priorities, not ad hoc.
PD impact is measured — teachers apply new learning and its effect on students is tracked.
Mentoring and coaching systems support early-career and developing teachers.
evidence_examples: Teacher self-evaluation forms (Edunav or equivalent)
Classroom observation records with developmental feedback
Professional development plan per teacher
PD attendance records and post-PD impact reflections
Mentee-mentor log books and check-in records

aspects.title: Cambridge Programme Quality & Compliance
aspects.description: The school delivers Cambridge programmes to the required standards and actively supports students' preparation for Cambridge examinations.
expectations: Cambridge entry, registration, and examination administration processes comply fully with Cambridge International requirements.
Students receive adequate curriculum time per Cambridge subject (as per syllabus guidance).
Predicted grades and internal assessments are submitted on time with appropriate moderation.
Examination results are analysed and findings fed back into curriculum and teaching decisions.
Staff have up-to-date Cambridge training (PDQ, TQ qualifications or equivalents).
evidence_examples: Cambridge Centre compliance checklists
Entry and results data with year-on-year trend analysis
Internal moderation records
Cambridge Examinations timetable and administration records
Cambridge PD certificates for teaching staff

aspects.title: Inclusion, Learning Support & EAL
aspects.description: The school identifies and supports students with diverse learning needs, including those with learning difficulties, English as an Additional Language (EAL) needs, and gifted and talented students.
expectations: The school has a written inclusion and learning support policy.
A process exists for identifying students with additional needs and documenting support plans.
EAL students receive targeted language support alongside academic content.
Gifted and talented students are challenged through extension tasks and enrichment opportunities.
Learning support interventions are monitored and their effectiveness reviewed regularly.
evidence_examples: Inclusion and learning support policy
Individual Education Plans (IEPs) or student support plans
EAL student tracking data and intervention records
Gifted and talented programme description and participation records
Learning support staff qualifications and deployment plan

domains.title: Student Achievement & Learning Outcomes
domains.description: Evaluates how well students are learning, how outcomes are tracked and used, and how the school supports and celebrates achievement across academic, co-curricular, and wellbeing dimensions.

aspects.title: Academic Performance & Benchmarking
aspects.description: The school tracks academic performance data systematically, benchmarks against Cambridge norms, and uses findings to drive improvement.
expectations: Cambridge IGCSE and A-Level results are analysed annually — pass rates, grade distributions, subject performance.
Results are benchmarked against Cambridge global averages and previous cohort data.
Subject-level and school-level performance targets are set at the start of each academic year.
Underperforming students are identified early and placed in targeted intervention programmes.
Academic performance data is shared with relevant stakeholders (staff, governors, Eduversal).
evidence_examples: Cambridge results analysis reports (current and previous 3 years)
Subject-level performance targets and end-of-year vs. target comparisons
Early identification and intervention records
Performance trend dashboards (grade distributions, pass rates by subject and cohort)
Reporting to governing body or foundation on academic outcomes

aspects.title: Assessment Design & Feedback Quality
aspects.description: Internal assessments are purposefully designed to measure deep learning. Feedback given to students is specific, developmental, and timely.
expectations: Internal assessments are designed with clear assessment objectives aligned to Cambridge standards.
Assessment tasks require higher-order thinking, not only recall.
Written and verbal feedback to students is specific, actionable, and linked to learning criteria.
Students have opportunities to respond to feedback and improve their work (closing the feedback loop).
The quality of feedback is monitored through book scrutiny or work sampling at least once per term.
evidence_examples: Sample assessment papers with mark schemes and examiner notes
Student work samples with teacher feedback annotations
Book scrutiny or work sampling records
Assessment quality review reports
Student feedback on assessment usefulness (survey results)

aspects.title: Individual Student Progress Monitoring
aspects.description: The school tracks individual student progress over time and uses the data to ensure every student reaches their potential.
expectations: Individual student progress data is maintained and reviewed at least once per term.
Students and parents receive regular progress reports that are informative and actionable.
Tutors or form teachers hold pastoral check-ins to discuss academic and personal progress.
Students at risk of underachievement are identified and placed on personalised support plans.
Student progress portfolios or records are maintained and accessible to relevant staff.
evidence_examples: Student progress tracking sheets or digital gradebook reports
Sample termly progress reports sent to parents
Pastoral meeting records and intervention logs
At-risk student support plans and outcome data
Student portfolios or e-portfolio records

aspects.title: Co-curricular & Extended Learning
aspects.description: The school provides meaningful co-curricular opportunities — including academic competitions, enrichment programmes, and student-led initiatives — that extend and deepen learning beyond the classroom.
expectations: A co-curricular programme is in place with a variety of academic, creative, sporting, and service activities.
The school participates in academic competitions (Olympiads, UNSW, Cambridge-aligned competitions) with a structured preparation programme.
Student participation rates in co-curricular activities are tracked and reported.
Competition and achievement records are maintained and celebrated.
Community service and student leadership opportunities are available and monitored.
evidence_examples: Co-curricular programme calendar and activity list
Olympiad and competition participation and achievement records
Student preparation programme documentation (tutor plans, schedules)
Co-curricular participation data by student and year group
Student leadership and community service records

aspects.title: Student Wellbeing & Engagement
aspects.description: The school actively supports student wellbeing, monitors engagement, and creates conditions where students thrive academically and personally.
expectations: A student wellbeing policy and programme is in place, addressing mental health, social-emotional learning, and physical health.
Attendance data is tracked regularly and low attendance is investigated and addressed promptly.
Student satisfaction and wellbeing surveys are conducted at least once per year and findings acted upon.
Anti-bullying and positive behaviour policies are implemented and reviewed.
Students have access to a counsellor or pastoral support officer.
evidence_examples: Student wellbeing policy and SEL programme documentation
Attendance tracking data and intervention records
Student wellbeing survey results and follow-up action report
Anti-bullying policy and incident log (anonymised)
Counsellor case records (anonymised) or referral statistics

domains.title: School Culture & Community
domains.description: Evaluates the quality of the school's culture, relationships with parents and the wider community, safeguarding practices, and the overall environment in which students, staff, and families operate.

aspects.title: School Culture & Staff Wellbeing
aspects.description: The school has a positive, collaborative, and purpose-driven culture. Staff wellbeing is prioritised and the school is a place where adults as well as students flourish.
expectations: A clearly defined set of values guides behaviour and decision-making across the school community.
Staff wellbeing is actively monitored through surveys and informal check-ins.
Workload management practices ensure staff sustainability (e.g. realistic meeting schedules, marking policies).
A culture of recognition exists — staff contributions are acknowledged formally and informally.
Staff turnover rate is monitored and low turnover is associated with high satisfaction data.
evidence_examples: School values documentation and how they are embedded in daily life
Staff wellbeing survey results and follow-up action report
Workload audit or staff feedback on workload sustainability
Staff recognition records or awards programme
Staff turnover data and exit interview insights

aspects.title: Parent & Family Engagement
aspects.description: The school actively involves parents as partners in education, maintaining transparent communication and creating meaningful opportunities for participation.
expectations: The school communicates regularly with parents through multiple channels (newsletter, app, parent portal, meetings).
Parent-teacher conferences are held at least twice per year and are structured and productive.
A parent satisfaction survey is conducted annually and results inform school actions.
Parents have a formal representative body (POMG / school committee) with a clear role.
Parents from diverse backgrounds feel welcomed and included in school life.
evidence_examples: Parent communication samples (newsletters, app messages, circulars)
Parent-teacher conference schedule and participation records
Parent satisfaction survey results and improvement actions
POMG / parent committee meeting minutes
Parent engagement events calendar and attendance records

aspects.title: Safeguarding & Child Protection
aspects.description: The school has robust safeguarding systems that protect the physical, emotional, and digital safety of all students at all times.
expectations: A Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL) is appointed with appropriate training.
A safeguarding and child protection policy is current, staff-trained, and reviewed annually.
All staff receive safeguarding training on induction and annually thereafter.
A clear and documented reporting and escalation procedure is in place and understood by all staff.
Site security, visitor management, and online safety protocols are implemented and monitored.
evidence_examples: Safeguarding policy and last review date
DSL appointment letter and training certificates
Staff safeguarding training attendance records
Reporting and escalation procedure flowchart
Site security log, visitor sign-in records, and online safety policy

aspects.title: Community Partnerships & External Engagement
aspects.description: The school builds productive relationships with the local community, universities, businesses, and other schools to enrich students' educational experiences and pathways.
expectations: The school has documented partnerships with at least three external organisations (universities, businesses, NGOs, professional bodies).
Partnership activities are linked to student learning outcomes (career exploration, university readiness, service learning).
Student university and career guidance is available from at least Year 10 onwards.
The school collaborates with other Eduversal partner schools through shared programmes or events.
External partnerships are reviewed annually for impact and relevance.
evidence_examples: Partnership agreements or MoUs
Partnership activity calendar and student participation records
University guidance programme schedule and materials
Inter-school collaboration event records
Partnership review meeting notes

aspects.title: Continuous Improvement & Quality Assurance
aspects.description: The school has a systematic approach to reviewing its own performance, using evidence to drive continuous improvement, and engaging all stakeholders in the quality assurance cycle.
expectations: A formal annual self-evaluation process is embedded in the school calendar.
Self-evaluation findings are shared with staff and governors, and used to set improvement priorities.
The school uses external reviews (Eduversal visits, CIS, Cambridge Centre feedback) as growth opportunities.
Improvement actions are SMART, resourced, and monitored for impact.
The school benchmarks its practices against national and international exemplars and adapts accordingly.
evidence_examples: Annual self-evaluation report
Governing body / foundation review meeting minutes discussing self-evaluation findings
External review reports and school responses / action plans
Improvement monitoring tracker showing progress against actions
Benchmarking activities or participation in learning networks
